Just now

--

Stop Creating Static Pages: How Dynamic Routing Works in Next.js App Router
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine you are building a blog or an e-commerce store with thousands of pages. Creating a separate folder and file for every single blog post or product is impossible.
This is where Dynamic Routing comes to the rescue. Instead of hardcoding paths, Next.js allows you to create a single “template” page that automatically adapts its content based on the URL the user visits.
Step 1: The Secret Sauce — Square Brackets [slug]
In Next.js, wrapping a folder name in square brackets creates a dynamic route. For example, [slug] acts like a placeholder or a variable.
Go to your app/blog/ directory, create a new folder named [slug], and add a page.tsx file inside it. Your folder structure must look like this:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 2: Grabbing the URL Data (The Code)
When a user visits [yourwebsite.com/blog/react-guide](https://yourwebsite.com/blog/react-guide), Next.js automatically passes react-guide as a parameter to your page component.
Here is how you catch that value and render it dynamically inside app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx:
Step 3: See the Magic in Action
Run your development server (npm run dev) and test these different URLs in your browser:

• localhost:3000/blog/nextjs ➡️Displays Nextjs

• localhost:3000/blog/mastering-python ➡️Displays Mastering Python
One single file ([slug]/page.tsx) now gracefully handles infinitely many unique links!
Conclusion
Dynamic routing is the backbone of modern web applications. By mastering [slug], you have unlocked the industry-standard way of building scalable, data-driven pages in Next.js without breaking a sweat.
Bonus: The 3 Types of Dynamic Routes in Industry
Next.js offers three ways to handle dynamic URLs depending on your project’s needs. Here is how the tech industry uses them:
1. Single Parameter: [slug] (Used 80% of the time)

• What it does: Matches exactly one URL segment.

• Industry Use Case: Perfect for flat structures like E-commerce product pages (/product/[id]), Social Media profiles (/[username]), and Blogs (/blog/[slug]).
2. Catch-All: [...slug] (Used 15% of the time)

• What it does: Matches multiple nested segments and returns them as an array.

• Industry Use Case: The king of E-commerce navigation and filtering. For example, a multi-category URL like /shop/men/shoes/nike is easily handled by a single [...slug] folder instead of creating dozens of sub-folders.
3. Optional Catch-All: [[...slug]] (Used 5% of the time)

• What it does: Matches multiple segments, but also works if the URL has zero extra segments (it is optional).

• Industry Use Case: Heavily used in Documentation and Wiki websites (like nextjs.org/docs). It allows developers to control both the main landing page (/docs) and deep inner pages (/docs/routing/layouts) using just one file.
